[![ThreatLocker](https://www.bleepstatic.com/c/t/tl-970.jpg)](https://www.threatlocker.com/because-a-fast-response-isnt-fast-enough?utm_source=bleeping_computer&utm_medium=sponsor&utm_campaign=not_fast_enough_financial_4-26&utm_content=not_fast_enough_financial-970x250&utm_term=display)

- [Home](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/)
- [News](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/)
- [Security](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/)
- New acoustic attack steals data from keystrokes with 95% accuracy

# New acoustic attack steals data from keystrokes with 95% accuracy

By

###### [Bill Toulas](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/author/bill-toulas/)

- August 5, 2023
- 10:09 AM
- [9](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/#comments)

![Keyboard](https://www.bleepstatic.com/content/hl-images/2023/08/04/mechanical-keyboard-glowing.jpg)

A team of researchers from British universities has trained a deep learning model that can steal data from keyboard keystrokes recorded using a microphone with an accuracy of 95%.

When Zoom was used for training the sound classification algorithm, the prediction accuracy dropped to 93%, which is still dangerously high, and a record for that medium.

Such an attack severely affects the target's data security, as it could leak people's passwords, discussions, messages, or other sensitive information to malicious third parties.

[![Wiz](https://www.bleepstatic.com/c/a/as-tour-the-platform-970-x250.jpg)](https://www.adaptivesecurity.com/demo/security-awareness-training?utm_source=display_network&utm_medium=paid_display&utm_campaign=2026_04_display_bleepingcomputer&utm_id=701Rd00000fE8REIA0&utm_content=970x250)

Moreover, contrary to other side-channel attacks that require special conditions and are subject to data rate and distance limitations, acoustic attacks have become much simpler due to the abundance of microphone-bearing devices that can achieve high-quality audio captures.

This, combined with the rapid advancements in machine learning, makes sound-based side-channel attacks feasible and a lot more dangerous than previously anticipated.

## Listening to keystrokes

The first step of the attack is to record keystrokes on the target's keyboard, as that data is required for training the prediction algorithm. This can be achieved via a nearby microphone or the target's phone that might have been infected by malware that has access to its microphone.

Alternatively, keystrokes can be recorded through a Zoom call where a rogue meeting participant makes correlations between messages typed by the target and their sound recording.

The researchers gathered training data by pressing 36 keys on a modern MacBook Pro 25 times each and recording the sound produced by each press.

![Sampling the keystroke audio](https://www.bleepstatic.com/images/news/u/1220909/2023/Papers/9/keystrokes-recording.jpg)**Sampling the keystroke audio** _(arxiv.org)_

Then, they produced waveforms and spectrograms from the recordings that visualize identifiable differences for each key and performed specific data processing steps to augment the signals that can be used for identifying keystrokes.

![Produced spectrograms](<Base64-Image-Removed>)**Produced spectrograms** _(arxiv.org)_

The spectrogram images were used to train 'CoAtNet,' which is an image classifier, while the process required some experimentation with epoch, learning rate, and data splitting parameters until the best prediction accuracy results could be achieved.

![Parameters selected for training CoAtNet](<Base64-Image-Removed>)**Parameters selected for training CoAtNet** _(arxiv.org)_

In their experiments, the researchers used the same laptop, whose keyboard has been used in all Apple laptops for the past two years, an iPhone 13 mini placed 17cm away from the target, and Zoom.

![The test setup](<Base64-Image-Removed>)**The test setup** _(arxiv.org)_

The CoANet classifier achieved 95% accuracy from the smartphone recordings and 93% from those captured through Zoom. Skype produced a lower but still usable 91.7% accuracy.

![The confusion matrix for phone-recorded keystrokes](<Base64-Image-Removed>)**Confusion matrix for phone-recorded keystrokes** _(arxiv.org)_

## Possible mitigations

For users who are overly worried about acoustic side-channel attacks, [the paper](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.01074.pdf) suggests that they may try altering typing styles or using randomized passwords.

Other potential defense measures include using software to reproduce keystroke sounds, white noise, or software-based keystroke audio filters.

Remember, the attack model proved highly effective even against a very silent keyboard, so adding sound dampeners on mechanical keyboards or switching to membrane-based keyboards is unlikely to help.

Ultimately, employing biometric authentication where feasible, and utilizing password managers to circumvent the need to input sensitive information manually, also serve as mitigating factors.

* * *

_Update 8/16_ \- A Zoom spokesperson has sent BleepingComputer the following comment on the above:

> Zoom takes the privacy and security of our users seriously.
>
> In addition to the mitigation techniques suggested by the researchers, Zoom users can also configure our background noise suppression feature to a higher setting, mute their microphone by default when joining a meeting, and mute their microphone when typing during a meeting to help keep their information more secure.

[![tines](<Base64-Image-Removed>)](https://hubs.li/Q048zztN0)

## [Automated Pentesting Covers Only 1 of 6 Surfaces.](https://hubs.li/Q048zztN0)

Automated pentesting proves the path exists. BAS proves whether your controls stop it. Most teams run one without the other.

This whitepaper maps six validation surfaces, shows where coverage ends, and provides practitioners with three diagnostic questions for any tool evaluation.

[Get Your Copy Now](https://hubs.li/Q048zztN0)

### Comments

- ![HughJazz Photo](<Base64-Image-Removed>)









###### [HughJazz](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/forums/u/1289966/hughjazz/) \- 2 years ago







  - [Report](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/# "Report")
  - [Link](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/#cid28110 "Link")

It isn't necessary to train AI for this type of attack. Each unique keystroke sound can be assigned it's own alphanumeric character. Decrypting a one for one single character encryption scheme in a known language is trivial.

- ![flubadub Photo](<Base64-Image-Removed>)









###### [flubadub](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/forums/u/1235387/flubadub/) \- 2 years ago

I look forward to your research paper

- ![Wagyourtail Photo](<Base64-Image-Removed>)









###### [Wagyourtail](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/forums/u/1290077/wagyourtail/) \- 2 years ago







  - [Report](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/# "Report")

[https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1102120.1102169](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1102120.1102169) literally from a 2005 research paper flubadub

- ![55304 Photo](<Base64-Image-Removed>)









###### [55304](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/forums/u/1246092/55304/) \- 2 years ago







  - [Report](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/# "Report")
  - [Link](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/#cid28124 "Link")

" [https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1102120.1102169](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1102120.1102169) literally from a 2005 research paper flubadub"

That research used a fixed dataset of 1,000 tunesian city names in arabic. Although impressive, it's hardly the same as what this article refers to.

[https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1109/TPAMI.2008.136](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1109/TPAMI.2008.136)

- ![h_b_s Photo](<Base64-Image-Removed>)









###### [h\_b\_s](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/forums/u/1211617/h-b-s/) \- 2 years ago







  - [Report](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/# "Report")
  - [Link](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/#cid28131 "Link")

""It isn't necessary to train AI for this type of attack. Each unique keystroke sound can be assigned it's own alphanumeric character. Decrypting a one for one single character encryption scheme in a known language is trivial. "

I look forward to your research paper"

No, OP is right. It's not necessary to use "AI" to do this. That's just a buzz word to make this sound like it was a unique find. The Ben-Gurion University in Israel showed this was possible many years ago. All these people did was train a ML algorithm to automate sorting through keyboard and typing styles audio snippets faster making the attack one or two steps closer to script kiddie level deployment. Unfortunately right now it's near impossible to find the Ben-Gurion team's work via search engine due to all the "me too" articles flooding search engines about this latest "issue" that security researchers and admins that HAVE BEEN PAYING ATTENTION already knew about (and one more reason why password managers are a Good Idea).

I'd point out that acoustic recording for exfiltration works on typewriters as well along with running back ribbon impressions, before someone decides to write up some OMG article about exfiltration on exfiltrating classified documents from certain government departments that utilize typewriters to avoid electronic attacks (DUH!)

- ![HughJazz Photo](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

  - [Link](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/#cid28111 "Link")

You are welcome, FBI and NSA.

  - [Link](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/#cid28132 "Link")

Who literally had nothing to do with this.

  - [Report](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/# "Report")

Zoom generally does a very good job of filtering out noises like keyboard strokes. Does the victim have to enable "original high quality sound" for this attack to work successfully over Zoom

- ![al3xa Photo](<Base64-Image-Removed>)









###### [al3xa](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/forums/u/1290475/al3xa/) \- 2 years ago







  - [Report](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-acoustic-attack-steals-data-from-keystrokes-with-95-percent-accuracy/# "Report")

By
Bill Toulas
  • August 5, 2023
  • 10:09 AM

A team of researchers from British universities has trained a deep learning model that can steal data from keyboard keystrokes recorded using a microphone with an accuracy of 95%.
